full_response:
We want one explicit change of coordinates x′μ(xν) for which the components obtained from the vector‐transformation law

v′μ  =  Σν (∂x′μ/∂xν) vν                               (1)

do NOT coincide with the actual new coordinates x′μ.  
If such a counter-example exists, the coordinates themselves cannot be the components of any genuine vector Xᵃ.

Step 1 : Pick a nonlinear coordinate transformation  
Keep three coordinates the same and square one of them:

x′0 = x0 ,  x′1 = (x1)² ,  x′2 = x2 ,  x′3 = x3.        (2)

(Here x0 ≡ t, x1 ≡ x, …; any nonlinear function would do, but the square is the simplest.)

Step 2 : Form the Jacobian ∂x′μ/∂xν  
Because (2) mixes only the 1–component non-linearly, the Jacobian matrix is

∂x′μ/∂xν  =  diag(1 , 2x1 , 1 , 1).                      (3)

Step 3 : Pretend the coordinates themselves are the components of a four-vector Xμ  
Apply the vector rule (1) with vμ ≡ Xμ = (x0 , x1 , x2 , x3):

X′1 (predicted)  =  Σν (∂x′1/∂xν) Xν
                 =  (∂x′1/∂x0) x0  +  (∂x′1/∂x1) x1
                 =  0·x0  +  (2x1)·x1
                 =  2 (x1)².                                (4)

Step 4 : Compare with the actual new coordinate  
From (2) the true coordinate is  

x′1 (actual)  =  (x1)².                                    (5)

Except at the single point x1 = 0, (4) ≠ (5); the factor of 2 (or, in general, any mismatch for a nonlinear change) shows the failure.

Because there exists at least one coordinate transformation for which the coordinates do NOT follow the vector transformation law, the set {xμ} cannot be the components of any vector Xᵃ.  Only differences of coordinates (dxμ) or tangent‐vector components transform as vectors.

\boxed{\text{Coordinates x^{\mu} do not transform as a vector; hence there is no four–vector }x^{a}.}

Confidence: 86%
